items.q: Are 'reuse' and 'recycle' the same thing in the 6 Rs?
items.correct: No — reuse means using an item again as it is or for a new purpose; recycling reprocesses the material into new products, which itself consumes energy.
items.misconception: Reusing and recycling are identical ways of dealing with waste.
items.random_wrong: Reuse means returning the product to the shop for resale.
items.why: Students merge the Rs; the hierarchy matters because reuse avoids the energy cost of reprocessing that recycling requires.

items.q: Does a product's environmental impact occur only when it is thrown away?
items.correct: No — life cycle assessment shows impacts at every stage: extraction, manufacture, transport, use and disposal; for appliances the use phase often dominates.
items.misconception: Products only harm the environment when they end up in landfill.
items.random_wrong: Environmental impact is measured purely by the size of the packaging.
items.why: Students fixate on visible waste; LCA thinking reveals hidden impacts like energy in use, which often outweigh disposal.

items.q: Does Fairtrade certification mean a product is environmentally friendly?
items.correct: Not necessarily — Fairtrade is primarily about fair prices and decent conditions for producers; it includes some standards but its core aim is social and economic.
items.misconception: A Fairtrade logo guarantees the product caused no environmental damage.
items.random_wrong: Fairtrade means the product was manufactured in the United Kingdom.
items.why: Students lump all ethical labels together; distinguishing social-justice aims from environmental ones earns evaluation marks.

items.q: What does a life cycle assessment evaluate?
items.correct: A product's environmental impact across its whole life
items.misconception: How long a battery lasts on one charge
items.random_wrong: The profitability of a product line
items.why: Correct answer: A product's environmental impact across its whole life. “How long a battery lasts on one charge” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In the 6 Rs, what does 'refuse' mean?
items.correct: Choosing not to buy or use unsustainable products or materials
items.random_wrong: Returning faulty goods for a refund
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Choosing not to buy or use unsustainable products or materials. “Throwing waste into landfill” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What does Fairtrade certification primarily ensure?
items.correct: Fair prices and conditions for producers
items.why: It's Fair prices and conditions for producers. “Zero packaging on products” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is a product's carbon footprint?
items.correct: The total greenhouse gas emissions associated with it
items.misconception: The floor space its factory occupies
items.random_wrong: The amount of carbon fibre it contains
items.why: It's The total greenhouse gas emissions associated with it. “The floor space its factory occupies” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
